questions.text: Polar bodies are formed during:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Somatic hybridization is a laboratory technique used to create hybrid cells or organisms by fusing two different somatic cells. It is not related to the formation of polar bodies.

Spermatogenesis is the process of producing sperm cells (spermatozoa) in the testes of males. During spermatogenesis, diploid spermatogonial cells undergo meiosis to form haploid sperm cells. It does not involve the formation of polar bodies.

Oogenesis is the process of producing ova (eggs) in the ovaries of females. During oogenesis, diploid oogonial cells undergo meiosis to form haploid eggs. Along with the formation of eggs, polar bodies are also produced. Polar bodies are small, non-functional cells that are generated during meiosis but do not develop into viable eggs. They are a byproduct of the unequal division of cytoplasm during oogenesis.

Spermiogenesis is the final stage of spermatogenesis, where the newly formed haploid spermatids mature into fully functional sperm cells (spermatozoa). It does not involve the formation of polar bodies.

questions.explanation: Oogenesis is the process of producing ova (eggs) in the ovaries of females. During oogenesis, diploid oogonial cells undergo meiosis to form haploid eggs. Along with the formation of eggs, polar bodies are also produced. Polar bodies are small, non-functional cells that are generated during meiosis but do not develop into viable eggs. They are a byproduct of the unequal division of cytoplasm during oogenesis.

questions.text: Nutrition to egg in ovary is provided by which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Germ cells refer to the cells that give rise to gametes (sperm in males and eggs in females). While germ cells are involved in the process of forming eggs, they are not the cells directly providing nutrition to the egg.
There is no specific cell type known as "milk cells" in the context of egg development in the ovary. The term is not applicable to this biological process.

Nutrition for the egg in the ovary is primarily provided by the follicle cells. These cells surround and support the developing egg within the ovarian follicle. They supply nutrients to the growing oocyte and help facilitate its maturation.
This option is not correct. Nutrition to the egg in the ovary is primarily provided by the follicle cells, not germ cells or imaginary "milk cells."

questions.explanation: Graafian follicle is a fluid-filled sac that surrounds the developing egg. It is located in the ovary and it is responsible for providing the egg with nutrients and protection.

questions.text: The period during which a girl sexually matures is called _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Menstrual cycle refers to the monthly cycle of changes that occur in a woman's body in preparation for pregnancy, including the shedding of the uterine lining and the release of an egg from the ovaries.
Puberty is the stage of adolescence where hormones for secondary sex characters are released and a girl sexually matures.
Childhood refers to the period of life before puberty.

Teens, or adolescence, is a broader term that refers to the period of life between childhood and adulthood, generally between the ages of 13 and 19, during which many significant physical, cognitive, and social changes occur.

questions.explanation: Puberty is the stage of adolescence where hormones for secondary sex characters are released and a girl sexually matures.

questions.text: What event occurs in the menstrual cycle when the level of progesterone declines?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ovulation is the release of an egg from the ovary, and it occurs in the middle of the menstrual cycle, typically around day 14 in a 28-day cycle. It is not directly associated with a decline in progesterone; in fact, ovulation is triggered by a surge in luteinizing hormone (LH) and is followed by an increase in progesterone.

When the level of progesterone declines in the menstrual cycle, it triggers the shedding of the uterine lining, leading to menstruation. This occurs if the released egg is not fertilized and there is no pregnancy. The decrease in progesterone allows the blood vessels in the uterus to constrict, leading to the detachment and expulsion of the uterine lining.

Menopause is a natural biological process that occurs in aging females, usually around the age of 45-55, and it signifies the end of menstrual cycles and fertility. It is not directly related to a decline in progesterone within an individual menstrual cycle.

Fertilization occurs when a sperm successfully combines with an egg. While fertilization can impact hormone levels in the longer term, it is not directly associated with the short-term decline in progesterone within the menstrual cycle.

questions.explanation: When the level of progesterone declines, it signals the beginning of the menstrual phase of the menstrual cycle. During the menstrual phase, the lining of the uterus (endometrium) breaks down and is shed.

questions.text: Which cells produce oogonia in ovary?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: A type of cell that makes up certain types of connective tissue is called stromal cell.
Epithelial cells are packed tightly together and serve as a barrier between the interior and the exterior of your body.

The cells that produce oogonia in the ovary are called primordial germ cells (PGCs). Primordial germ cells migrate to the developing ovaries during embryonic development and give rise to oogonia. Oogonia undergo several rounds of mitosis to increase their numbers. However, before birth, the oogonia enter a state of meiotic arrest and become primary oocytes.
Theca cells are the endocrine cells associated with ovarian follicles that play an essential role in fertility by producing the androgen substrate required for ovarian estrogen biosynthesis.

questions.explanation: The cells that produce oogonia in the ovary are called primordial germ cells (PGCs). Primordial germ cells migrate to the developing ovaries during embryonic development and give rise to oogonia. Oogonia undergo several rounds of mitosis to increase their numbers. However, before birth, the oogonia enter a state of meiotic arrest and become primary oocytes.

questions.text: During birth which of following act as birth canal?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Oviduct is a tube through which an ovum or egg passes from an ovary.
The ovaries are small, oval-shaped glands located on either side of your uterus. They produce and store eggs (also called ovum) and make hormones that control menstrual cycle and pregnancy.
The organ in the lower body of a woman or female mammal where offspring are conceived and in which they gestate before birth is known as the uterus or womb.
During birth, the birth canal is formed by the passage through which the baby travels during delivery. The muscular canal that goes from the uterus to the outside of the body is known as vagina.

questions.explanation: During birth, the birth canal is formed by the passage through which the baby travels during delivery. The vagina is the muscular tube that connects the cervix to the external genitalia. It serves as the main part of the birth canal through which the baby passes during delivery.

questions.text: Another name for the sex cell is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hormones are chemical messengers produced by glands in the endocrine system. They regulate various physiological processes in the body but are not synonymous with sex cells.
A gamete is a sex cell, either a sperm (male gamete) or an egg (female gamete). Gametes carry genetic information and are involved in sexual reproduction.
A zygote is formed when two gametes (sperm and egg) fuse during fertilization. It represents the initial stage of a developing organism.
A testicle is an organ in the male reproductive system that produces sperm and hormones. While it is involved in the production of gametes (sperm), "gamete" is the more general term for a sex cell.

questions.explanation: Sex cell are called reproductive cells or gametes.

questions.text: Secondary oocyte is ovulated from:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The corpus luteum is a vital yet temporary organ that plays a crucial role in fertility during the luteal phase.

The secondary oocyte is ovulated from a structure known as the Graafian follicle, also referred to as the mature or tertiary follicle. The process starts with the development of a primordial follicle, which goes through several stages of development, including the primary follicle, secondary follicle, and then the mature Graafian follicle. The Graafian follicle is the final stage of follicular development before ovulation. Upon maturation, the Graafian follicle releases the secondary oocyte during ovulation. This event marks the rupture of the follicle, and the secondary oocyte is released into the fallopian tube. From there, it can be fertilized by sperm if present, leading to the formation of a zygote and, ultimately, pregnancy. If fertilization does not occur, the remaining components of the Graafian follicle undergo degeneration.

The primary follicle is defined by the presence of one or more cuboidal cells that are arranged in a single layer surrounding the oocyte. The major developmental events that occur in the primary follicle include FSH receptor expression and oocyte growth and differentiation.
The germinal epithelium is the epithelial layer of the seminiferous tubules of the testicles. It is also known as the wall of the seminiferous tubules.

questions.explanation: The secondary oocyte is ovulated from a structure known as the Graafian follicle, also referred to as the mature or tertiary follicle. The process starts with the development of a primordial follicle, which goes through several stages of development, including the primary follicle, secondary follicle, and then the mature Graafian follicle. The Graafian follicle is the final stage of follicular development before ovulation. Upon maturation, the Graafian follicle releases the secondary oocyte during ovulation. This event marks the rupture of the follicle, and the secondary oocyte is released into the fallopian tube. From there, it can be fertilized by sperm if present, leading to the formation of a zygote and, ultimately, pregnancy. If fertilization does not occur, the remaining components of the Graafian follicle undergo degeneration.

questions.text: Which hormone is produced mainly by corpus luteum in the ovary following ovulation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The hormone produced mainly by the corpus luteum in the ovary following ovulation is progesterone. The corpus luteum forms from the ruptured follicle that released an egg during ovulation. After ovulation, the empty follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, and its primary function is to produce progesterone.
In women, FSH helps manage the menstrual cycle and stimulates the ovaries to produce eggs.

In males, LH stimulates testosterone release by the Leydig cells of the testes. In females, LH stimulates steroid release from the ovaries, ovulation, and the release of progesterone after ovulation by the corpus luteum.

Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) is a hormone produced by the placenta during pregnancy. It's sometimes called the pregnancy hormone because of its unique role in supporting a pregnancy. HCG is found in your urine or blood around 10 to 11 days after conception.

questions.explanation: The hormone produced mainly by the corpus luteum in the ovary following ovulation is progesterone. The corpus luteum forms from the ruptured follicle that releases an egg during ovulation. After ovulation, the empty follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, and its primary function is to produce progesterone.

questions.text: The proximal part of the oviduct is significant because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The oviduct or Fallopian tube is the anatomical region where every new life begins in mammalian species. After a long journey, the spermatozoa meet the oocyte in the specific site of the oviduct named ampulla, and fertilization takes place.

This option is incorrect. The infundibulum and the entire oviduct play a critical role in the early stages of reproduction by capturing the egg, facilitating fertilization, and supporting the transport of the developing embryo toward the uterus for implantation.

This in incorrect. The infundibulum and the entire oviduct play a critical role in the early stages of reproduction by capturing the egg, facilitating fertilization, and supporting the transport of the developing embryo toward the uterus for implantation.
This option is incorrect as the correct option is A.

questions.explanation: The proximal part of the oviduct, also known as the infundibulum, is significant because it plays a crucial role in capturing the released egg (ovum) from the ovary and facilitating its transport into the oviduct. The oviduct is the site where fertilization typically occurs. If sperm is present in the female reproductive tract, it can meet the egg in the oviduct. Fertilization involves the fusion of a sperm cell with the egg, resulting in the formation of a zygote. The infundibulum and the entire oviduct play a critical role in the early stages of reproduction by capturing the egg, facilitating fertilization, and supporting the transport of the developing embryo toward the uterus for implantation.

questions.text: The umbilical cord contains which of the following:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Pluripotent stem cells are versatile but are typically found in early embryos, not specifically in the umbilical cord.
Blood stem cells are indeed found in the umbilical cord, but this term alone does not fully describe the specific type found there.
Cord blood stem cells are specifically derived from the blood of the umbilical cord and are used in regenerative therapies and transplants.

options.text: Both Options A and B are correct
options.explanation: While both pluripotent and blood stem cells are valuable, pluripotent stem cells are not specifically characteristic of the umbilical cord's contents.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is cord blood stem cells. The umbilical cord contains blood stem cells, specifically referred to as cord blood stem cells, which are harvested from the cord blood. These cells are important due to their potential for use in medical treatments, such as regenerative therapies and transplants. Pluripotent stem cells, while capable of differentiating into any cell type, are generally associated with embryonic sources, not the umbilical cord. Therefore, the presence of pluripotent stem cells is not characteristic of the umbilical cord's contents, making Option A incorrect. Similarly, while blood stem cells are present, the term 'cord blood stem cells' is more specific and accurate, which is why Option C is the correct choice. Option D is incorrect because it suggests the presence of pluripotent stem cells in the umbilical cord, which is misleading.

questions.text: The follicle celIs, after release of the egg, are modified to form a special structure called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The endometrium is the tissue that lines the uterus, or wom the pear-shaped organ that houses a fetus. This mucous membrane thickens in anticipation of a possible pregnancy. The endometrium thus plays a pivotal role in reproduction and continuation of our species.

The perimetrium is the outer serous layer of the uterus. The serous layer secretes a lubricating fluid that helps to reduce friction. The perimetrium is also part of the peritoneum that covers some of the organs of the pelvis.

After the release of the egg (ovulation) from the ovarian follicle, the remaining structure transforms into a specialized endocrine organ called the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum plays a crucial role in the menstrual and reproductive cycles. It produces progesterone, along with some estrogen, which helps prepare the uterine lining (endometrium) for a potential pregnancy. If fertilization occurs and the embryo implants in the uterus, the corpus luteum is maintained, and it continues to produce hormones to support early pregnancy.

options.text: None of these options are correct
options.explanation: This is the incorrect option as option C is correct.

questions.explanation: After the release of the egg (ovulation) from the ovarian follicle, the remaining structure transforms into a specialized endocrine organ called the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum plays a crucial role in the menstrual and reproductive cycles. It produces progesterone, along with some estrogen, which helps prepare the uterine lining (endometrium) for a potential pregnancy. If fertilization occurs and the embryo implants in the uterus, the corpus luteum is maintained, and it continues to produce hormones to support early pregnancy.

questions.text: The event happens in menstrual cycle when level of progesterone declines:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ovulation occurs when level of LH increases and ovulation causes increase in progesterone level.
After ovulation, corpus luteum is formed which increases progesterone level and maintains it .

Progesterone is essential to maintaining the lining of the endometrium. When its levels fall at the end of the menstrual cycle, the endometrium lining sheds leading to menstruation/beginning of menses.

Normal female fertility relies on proper development of the oocyte. This growth culminates just prior to ovulation, when oocyte maturation occurs. Oocyte maturation refers to a release of meiotic arrest that allows oocytes to advance from prophase I to metaphase II of meiosis

questions.explanation: Progesterone is essential to maintaining the lining of the endometrium. When its levels fall at the end of the menstrual cycle, the endometrium lining sheds leading to menstruation/beginning of menses. Menstruation, or the menstrual phase, typically occurs around Days 1-5 of the menstrual cycle. It represents the start of a new menstrual cycle if pregnancy has not occurred. The decline in progesterone is a key factor in initiating the physiological changes that result in the shedding of the uterine lining during menstruation.

questions.text: After fertilization the zygote increases in size and travels down the fallopian tube to become embedded in the walls of the womb. This process is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Ovulation refers to the process during which a mature egg (ovum) is released from the ovary into the fallopian tube. It occurs roughly in the middle of a woman’s menstrual cycle. Ovulation happens before fertilization, not after it. Once the egg is released, it can be fertilized by a sperm cell, but ovulation itself has nothing to do with the zygote or its movement to the uterus.

This option is correct. After fertilization occurs in the fallopian tube, the resulting zygote undergoes several mitotic divisions to form a blastocyst (a hollow ball of cells). This blastocyst then travels down the fallopian tube to reach the uterus (womb). Once there, it attaches itself to and embeds within the endometrial lining of the uterus — a process known as implantation. Implantation marks the beginning of pregnancy, as it allows the developing embryo to receive oxygen and nutrients from the mother’s blood through the uterine lining.

This option is partially correct but not the best answer. Conception refers to the moment of fertilization, when a sperm cell successfully penetrates and fuses with an egg cell in the fallopian tube to form a zygote. However, the question describes the process after fertilization, when the zygote becomes embedded in the uterine wall. Therefore, the correct term for that stage is implantation, not conception.

This option is incorrect. Menstruation (period) is the shedding of the uterine lining (endometrium) when fertilization and implantation do not occur. It marks the end of one menstrual cycle and the beginning of another. Menstruation is the opposite of implantation, as it occurs when there is no pregnancy.

questions.explanation: After fertilization, the zygote undergoes a series of cell divisions, forming a structure known as a blastocyst. The blastocyst then travels down the fallopian tube and eventually reaches the uterus. The process by which the blastocyst becomes embedded in the walls of the uterus is called implantation. Implantation generally occurs around 6-10 days after fertilization. The embryo, at this stage, is a multicellular structure, and the process of implantation marks the beginning of the embryonic stage of development. Once implanted, the cells of the blastocyst continue to differentiate, and the early structures of the developing fetus begin to form.

questions.text: In mammals that are seasonal breeders, females are receptive only once a year. This is known as:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The follicular phase, also known as the pre-ovulatory phase or proliferative phase, is the phase of the estrous cycle during which follicles in the ovary mature from primary follicle to a fully mature graafian follicle. It ends with ovulation.

In mammals that are seasonal breeders, females are receptive to mating only once a year during a specific period. This behavior is known as "seasonal estrus" or "seasonal heat." During the receptive period, females exhibit behaviors and physiological changes indicating their readiness to mate. The seasonal estrus, also spelled as the oestrous cycle, is the reproductive cycle that occurs in most mammalian females, excluding humans and certain primates. It consists of a series of hormonal and physiological changes that prepare the female for reproduction.
The luteal phase of menstrual cycle occurs right after ovulation (when ovary releases an egg). It lasts about 14 days and ends when menstrual period starts.

The menstrual cycle is a natural process. It is a complex cycle controlled by female hormones that cause regular bleeding (periods). The menstrual cycle has four phases: menstruation, the follicular phase, ovulation and the luteal phase. Some women may experience menstrual problems (e.g. heavy bleeding).

questions.explanation: In mammals that are seasonal breeders, females are receptive to mating only once a year during a specific period. This behavior is known as "seasonal estrus" or "seasonal heat." During the receptive period, females exhibit behaviors and physiological changes indicating their readiness to mate. The seasonal estrus, also spelled as the oestrous cycle, is the reproductive cycle that occurs in most mammalian females, excluding humans and certain primates. It consists of a series of hormonal and physiological changes that prepare the female for reproduction.

questions.text: Which term refers to the formation of egg cells that begins in the developing ovaries of a female fetus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Meiosis is a special type of cell division of germ cells in sexually-reproducing organisms that produces the gametes, such as sperm or egg cells. It involves two rounds of division that ultimately result in four cells with only one copy of each chromosome.
Fertilization is defined as the union of two gametes. During fertilization, sperm and egg fuse to form a diploid zygote to initiate prenatal development.
Release of egg from ovum is called ovulation.

The term that refers to the formation of egg cells (ova) that begins in the developing ovaries of a female fetus is "oogenesis." Oogenesis is the process by which ova (egg cells) are produced. It starts during fetal development and continues throughout the reproductive years of a female's life.

questions.explanation: The term that refers to the formation of egg cells (ova) that begins in the developing ovaries of a female fetus is "oogenesis." Oogenesis is the process by which ova (egg cells) are produced. It starts during fetal development and continues throughout the reproductive years of a female's life.

questions.text: During birth which of following act as birth canal?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tube through which an ovum or egg passes from an ovary is called oviduct.

Ovary is one of a pair of female glands in which the eggs form and the female hormones estrogen and progesterone are made. These hormones play an important role in female traits, such as breast development, body shape, and body hair. They are also involved in the menstrual cycle, fertility, and pregnancy.
The hollow, pear-shaped organ in a woman's pelvis is uterus. The uterus is where a fetus (unborn baby) develops and grows.

The vagina is a muscular canal that extends from the vulva to the neck of the uterus (cervix). The vagina is where the lining of the uterus is shed during menstruation, where penetration can occur during sex and where a baby descends during childbirth. So, it acts as a birth canal.

questions.explanation: During birth, the birth canal refers to the pathway through which the baby passes from the uterus to the outside world. In humans, the birth canal is composed of two main structures: Cervix: The cervix is the lower, narrow part of the uterus that connects to the vagina. During labor, the cervix dilates (opens) to allow the baby to pass through. Vagina: The vagina is the muscular tube that extends from the cervix to the external genitalia. It serves as the passageway for the baby during delivery. As labor progresses, contractions of the uterus and the muscles surrounding the birth canal help push the baby through the cervix and into the vagina, ultimately leading to childbirth. The process of labor and delivery is a complex physiological event that involves the coordinated efforts of both the mother's body and the baby's positioning for a successful passage through the birth canal.

questions.text: During the menstrual cycle, which of the following events happens if a released egg does not become fertilized?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The lining of the womb wall stays built up
options.explanation: This option is not correct because the lining of the womb wall, also known as the endometrium, breaks down during menstruation.

options.text: The lining of the womb wall builds up again
options.explanation: This option is also not correct because the lining of the womb wall needs to break down first before it can build up again in preparation for a potential pregnancy.
This option is also not correct because the release of an egg from the ovary, also known as ovulation, typically occurs once per menstrual cycle, regardless of whether or not the egg is fertilized.

options.text: The lining of the womb wall breaks down
options.explanation: During the menstrual cycle, if a released egg does not become fertilized, the lining of the womb wall breaks down. This is known as menstruation and marks the beginning of a new menstrual cycle.

questions.explanation: During the menstrual cycle, if a released egg does not become fertilized, the lining of the womb wall breaks down. This is known as menstruation and marks the beginning of a new menstrual cycle.

questions.text: The female gametes are most commonly refered as?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The term “egg” is the most widely used common name for the female gamete in animals, including humans. In biological terms, the egg is a large, non-motile reproductive cell produced by the female reproductive organ known as the ovary. The process of egg formation is called oogenesis, which begins before a female’s birth and continues throughout her reproductive life. Each egg contains half the number of chromosomes (haploid set) needed to form a new organism. When an egg fuses with a male gamete (sperm) during fertilization, the result is a zygote — the first cell of a new individual. The egg provides not only the mother’s genetic contribution but also the nutritional material and cellular components necessary to begin embryonic development. Thus, the term “egg” correctly refers to the female gamete in a general sense.

The term “ova” (plural form of ovum) is a more scientific or biological term used to describe female gametes. Like “eggs,” ova are produced in the ovaries during oogenesis. Each ovum is a haploid cell, meaning it contains 23 chromosomes in humans — half the number found in normal body cells. During fertilization, when an ovum fuses with a sperm cell, the zygote formed restores the diploid number of chromosomes (46 in humans). The ovum is significantly larger than the sperm, containing cytoplasm rich in nutrients that support the early stages of embryonic growth. Therefore, “ova” is also a correct term for female gametes, especially in scientific contexts.

The term “ovum” is the singular form of “ova” and specifically refers to one individual female gamete. Just like the plural term, “ovum” is produced by the ovaries and is involved in the reproductive process. An ovum is a non-motile, spherical cell surrounded by a protective layer called the zona pellucida, which plays an essential role during fertilization by allowing only one sperm to enter. In humans, a single ovum is typically released during each menstrual cycle — a process known as ovulation. If fertilization occurs, the ovum contributes the maternal half of the genetic material to the developing zygote. Hence, the term “ovum” is scientifically accurate and commonly used to describe the female gamete.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: This is the correct answer, as all three terms — egg, ova, and ovum — are valid and commonly used to describe the female gamete. The word “egg” is the simplest and most general term, used in everyday language; “ova” is the plural scientific form, while “ovum” is the singular scientific term. Regardless of terminology, all three refer to the same cell type responsible for carrying the female’s genetic information and participating in fertilization to form a new organism. Therefore, each of the above terms correctly identifies the female reproductive cell, making option D the most accurate and comprehensive choice.

questions.explanation: Female gametes are most commonly referred to as eggs or ova (singular: ovum). These cells are produced by the ovaries through a process called oogenesis. The female gamete carries half of the genetic material required for reproduction, and it fuses with a male gamete (sperm) during fertilization to form a zygote, which eventually develops into an embryo.

questions.text: The event happens in the menstrual cycle when the level of progesterone declines is?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Ovulation is the release of an egg from the ovary, and it occurs midway through the menstrual cycle. It is not directly associated with a decline in progesterone; in fact, ovulation is triggered by a surge in luteinizing hormone (LH) and is followed by an increase in progesterone.

The event that happens in the menstrual cycle when the level of progesterone declines is the beginning of menses, also known as menstruation. The decrease in progesterone triggers the shedding of the uterine lining, leading to the discharge of blood and tissue from the uterus.

The corpus luteum is formed from the remnants of the ovarian follicle after ovulation. It produces progesterone, which helps prepare the uterus for a potential pregnancy. The decline of the corpus luteum and progesterone is associated with the beginning of menses.
The maturation of the ovarian follicle occurs before ovulation when the egg is released from the ovary. It is not directly associated with the decline in progesterone.

questions.explanation: Progesterone is the primary hormone in the maintenance of the endometrium lining. If the egg is not fertilised, progesterone level declines, the uterine wall starts breaking down and the menses start.

questions.text: In human females, the release of the ovum from the follicle is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The release of the ovum from the graafian follicle is called ovulation. Therefore, this is the correct option.
Fertilization is when the sperm and egg fuses. Therefore, is not the correct option.
Menstruation is monthly vaginal bleeding that is a part of the woman cycle. Thus, this is the correct option.
Implantation is when a fertilized cell attaches to the uterine lining.

questions.explanation: The release of the ovum (egg) from the follicle in human females is called ovulation. Ovulation is a key event in the menstrual cycle and typically occurs around the middle of the menstrual cycle, roughly 14 days before the start of the next menstrual period. During ovulation, a mature follicle in the ovary ruptures, releasing the egg into the fallopian tube, where it can potentially be fertilized by sperm. If fertilization does not occur, the egg will disintegrate, and the menstrual cycle will proceed with the shedding of the uterine lining during menstruation.

questions.text: During birth which of following act as birth canal?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The tube through which an ovum or egg passes from an ovary is called oviduct.

Ovary is one of a pair of female glands in which the eggs form and the female hormones estrogen and progesterone are made. These hormones play an important role in female traits, such as breast development, body shape, and body hair. They are also involved in the menstrual cycle, fertility, and pregnancy.
The hollow, pear-shaped organ in a woman's pelvis is uterus. The uterus is where a fetus (unborn baby) develops and grows.

The vagina is a muscular canal that extends from the vulva to the neck of the uterus (cervix). The vagina is where the lining of the uterus is shed during menstruation, where penetration can occur during sex and where a baby descends during childbirth. So, it acts as a birth canal.

questions.explanation: During birth, the birth canal refers to the pathway through which the baby passes from the uterus to the outside world. In humans, the birth canal is composed of two main structures: Cervix: The cervix is the lower, narrow part of the uterus that connects to the vagina. During labor, the cervix dilates (opens) to allow the baby to pass through. Vagina: The vagina is the muscular tube that extends from the cervix to the external genitalia. It serves as the passageway for the baby during delivery. As labor progresses, contractions of the uterus and the muscles surrounding the birth canal help push the baby through the cervix and into the vagina, ultimately leading to childbirth. The process of labor and delivery is a complex physiological event that involves the coordinated efforts of both the mother's body and the baby's positioning for a successful passage through the birth canal.

questions.text: Which one of the following statements is incorrect?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Eggs in the ovaries ripen when they meet a sperm
options.explanation: Incorrect: Eggs in the ovaries are fully developed when released. They do not need to meet a sperm to ripen. Fertilization, not ripening, occurs if an egg meets a sperm.

options.text: Girls are born with thousands of eggs in their ovaries
options.explanation: Correct: Females are born with a finite number of immature eggs called primary oocytes. These eggs are present at birth and do not increase in number during a woman's lifetime.

options.text: Hormones control the release of the egg from the ovary
options.explanation: Correct: Hormones such as luteinizing hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH) regulate the menstrual cycle and control the release of eggs from the ovaries.

options.text: One egg is released from the ovary about every month
options.explanation: Correct: Typically, one egg is released from the ovary each month during the process of ovulation, which is part of the menstrual cycle.

questions.explanation: Eggs in the ovaries are already fully developed when they are released. They do not need to meet a sperm in order to ripen. Once an egg is released from the ovary, it can be fertilized by a sperm and become a baby. If an egg is not fertilized, it will break down and results in mensturation.

questions.text: To overcome infertility, which technique is used:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This is the process in which the lady receives fertility medication through an injection at regular intervals of time for a minimum of 6 weeks. These medicines allow and ensure that an egg is matured for reproduction. Once it is ready, it is harvested and put into a test tube. Same day the sperm is collected and then they are fused to form a zygote. This zygote undergoes incubation for observation. Then it is placed into the female uterus. She takes continuous medication to support the fetus throughout the pregnancy.

This is the natural method for obtaining pregnancy. As a couple has sexual intercourse, it allows the sperm to travel from the vagina to the cervix. They then reach the fallopian tubes where they fertilize the egg and the zygote divides by mitosis to grow. It then implants itself into the uterine wall. This option is wrong because it can naturally occur and does not help to overcome infertility.

This option is incorrect because only in vitro fertilization (option A) is a medically assisted technique used to overcome infertility. In vivo fertilization is natural and not a medical intervention. Hence, both cannot be correct together.
This option is incorrect because option A (In vitro fertilization) is indeed a valid and effective technique to treat infertility. Therefore, “None of these” is not applicable.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct as this is the process in which the lady recieves fertility medication through an injection at regular intervals of time for minimum 6 weeks. These medicine allow and ensure that a egg is matured for reproduction. Once it is ready, it is harvested and put into a test tube. Same day the sperm is collected and then they are fused to form a zygote. This zygote undergoes incubation for observation. Then it is placed into the female uterus. She takes continous medication to support the fetus throughout the pregnancy.

questions.text: The follicle cells, after release of the egg, are modified to form a special structure called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The endometrium is the inner lining of the uterus and is not formed from follicle cells. It is unrelated to the transformation that occurs after the egg is released.
The perimetrium is the outermost layer of the uterus and has no connection to the transformation of follicle cells post-ovulation.

After ovulation, the follicle cells transform into the corpus luteum, a structure crucial for secreting hormones like progesterone, which are vital for supporting the uterine lining and potential pregnancy.
This is incorrect as the correct structure formed from follicle cells after egg release is the corpus luteum.

questions.explanation: After the release of the egg (ovulation) from a mature ovarian follicle, the remaining follicle cells undergo a transformation to form a structure known as the corpus luteum. The corpus luteum plays a crucial role in the menstrual cycle and early pregnancy. The corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine structure that develops from the ruptured follicle. It secretes hormones, primarily progesterone and some estrogen, which are essential for the maintenance of the uterine lining (endometrium) during the second half of the menstrual cycle. These hormones help prepare the uterus for a potential pregnancy.

questions.text: Placenta is important as its function is to:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The placenta facilitates the exchange of oxygen from the mother's blood to the fetus, ensuring the developing baby receives the oxygen it needs for growth.
Similarly, the placenta allows for the removal of carbon dioxide from the fetal blood, assisting in the elimination of waste products produced by the developing baby.

The placenta is responsible for transferring essential nutrients, such as glucose and amino acids, from the mother's bloodstream to the fetus, providing the necessary sustenance for fetal development.

The placenta plays a crucial role in the exchange of oxygen, carbon dioxide, and nutrients between the maternal and fetal circulations, supporting the overall well-being and growth of the developing fetus.

questions.explanation: The placenta plays a crucial role in the exchange of oxygen, carbon dioxide, and nutrients between the maternal and fetal circulations, supporting the overall well-being and growth of the developing fetus.

questions.text: The proximal part of the oviduct is significant because:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The oviduct or Fallopian tube is the anatomical region where every new life begins in mammalian species. After a long journey, the spermatozoa meet the oocyte in the specific site of the oviduct named ampulla, and fertilization takes place.

This option is incorrect. The infundibulum and the entire oviduct play a critical role in the early stages of reproduction by capturing the egg, facilitating fertilization, and supporting the transport of the developing embryo toward the uterus for implantation.

This in incorrect. The infundibulum and the entire oviduct play a critical role in the early stages of reproduction by capturing the egg, facilitating fertilization, and supporting the transport of the developing embryo toward the uterus for implantation.
This option is incorrect as the correct option is A.

questions.explanation: The proximal part of the oviduct, also known as the infundibulum, is significant because it plays a crucial role in capturing the released egg (ovum) from the ovary and facilitating its transport into the oviduct. The oviduct is the site where fertilization typically occurs. If sperm is present in the female reproductive tract, it can meet the egg in the oviduct. Fertilization involves the fusion of a sperm cell with the egg, resulting in the formation of a zygote. The infundibulum and the entire oviduct play a critical role in the early stages of reproduction by capturing the egg, facilitating fertilization, and supporting the transport of the developing embryo toward the uterus for implantation.

questions.text: The female gametes are most commonly refered as?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The term “egg” is the most widely used common name for the female gamete in animals, including humans. In biological terms, the egg is a large, non-motile reproductive cell produced by the female reproductive organ known as the ovary. The process of egg formation is called oogenesis, which begins before a female’s birth and continues throughout her reproductive life. Each egg contains half the number of chromosomes (haploid set) needed to form a new organism. When an egg fuses with a male gamete (sperm) during fertilization, the result is a zygote — the first cell of a new individual. The egg provides not only the mother’s genetic contribution but also the nutritional material and cellular components necessary to begin embryonic development. Thus, the term “egg” correctly refers to the female gamete in a general sense.

The term “ova” (plural form of ovum) is a more scientific or biological term used to describe female gametes. Like “eggs,” ova are produced in the ovaries during oogenesis. Each ovum is a haploid cell, meaning it contains 23 chromosomes in humans — half the number found in normal body cells. During fertilization, when an ovum fuses with a sperm cell, the zygote formed restores the diploid number of chromosomes (46 in humans). The ovum is significantly larger than the sperm, containing cytoplasm rich in nutrients that support the early stages of embryonic growth. Therefore, “ova” is also a correct term for female gametes, especially in scientific contexts.

The term “ovum” is the singular form of “ova” and specifically refers to one individual female gamete. Just like the plural term, “ovum” is produced by the ovaries and is involved in the reproductive process. An ovum is a non-motile, spherical cell surrounded by a protective layer called the zona pellucida, which plays an essential role during fertilization by allowing only one sperm to enter. In humans, a single ovum is typically released during each menstrual cycle — a process known as ovulation. If fertilization occurs, the ovum contributes the maternal half of the genetic material to the developing zygote. Hence, the term “ovum” is scientifically accurate and commonly used to describe the female gamete.

options.text: All of these options are correct
options.explanation: This is the correct answer, as all three terms — egg, ova, and ovum — are valid and commonly used to describe the female gamete. The word “egg” is the simplest and most general term, used in everyday language; “ova” is the plural scientific form, while “ovum” is the singular scientific term. Regardless of terminology, all three refer to the same cell type responsible for carrying the female’s genetic information and participating in fertilization to form a new organism. Therefore, each of the above terms correctly identifies the female reproductive cell, making option D the most accurate and comprehensive choice.

questions.explanation: Female gametes are most commonly referred to as eggs or ova (singular: ovum). These cells are produced by the ovaries through a process called oogenesis. The female gamete carries half of the genetic material required for reproduction, and it fuses with a male gamete (sperm) during fertilization to form a zygote, which eventually develops into an embryo.

questions.text: The oviduct is generally called the:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the oviduct is not only called the fallopian tube but it is also called the uterine tube.
This option is incorrect because the oviduct is not only called the uterine tube but it is also called the fallopian tube.

options.text: Both A and B options are correct
options.explanation: Oviduct is also known as fallopian tube and uterine tube in human species. The fallopian tubes are a pair of structures that connect the ovaries to the uterus in the female reproductive system. They play a crucial role in the transport of eggs (ova) from the ovaries to the uterus and provide the site where fertilization typically occurs if sperm is present.
Uterus is the hollow, pear-shaped organ in a woman's pelvis. The uterus is where a fetus (unborn baby) develops and grows. It is also called womb.

questions.explanation: The oviduct is generally called the fallopian tube or uterine tube. The fallopian tubes are a pair of structures that connect the ovaries to the uterus in the female reproductive system. They play a crucial role in the transport of eggs (ova) from the ovaries to the uterus and provide the site where fertilization typically occurs if sperm is present.

questions.text: Which factors affect the female reproductive cycle?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Malnourishment and inadequate nutrition can impact the female reproductive cycle by disrupting hormone production and affecting the regularity of menstrual cycles.
Emotional stress can influence the female reproductive cycle by disrupting the normal hormonal balance, potentially leading to irregularities in the menstrual cycle.

Both malnourishment and emotional stress can adversely affect the female reproductive cycle. Nutritional deficiencies and emotional factors can disrupt the intricate hormonal regulation of the menstrual cycle.
This option is not correct, as both malnourishment and emotional stress are recognized factors that can impact the female reproductive cycle.

questions.explanation: Both malnourishment and emotional stress can adversely affect the female reproductive cycle. Nutritional deficiencies and emotional factors can disrupt the intricate hormonal regulation of the menstrual cycle.

questions.text: Which is an organelle that helps the sperm to penetrate the ovum?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Megalis tablet is used to treat erectile dysfunction in men. It increases blood flow to the penis to help men get an erection. It works by helping to relax the blood vessels in the penis.
The zona pellucida is a relatively thick extracellular matrix (ECM) that surrounds all mammalian oocytes, called eggs.

The organelle that plays a crucial role in helping the sperm penetrate the ovum (egg) during fertilization is the acrosome. The acrosome is a specialized structure located at the tip of the sperm cell's head. The acrosome is a vesicle filled with hydrolytic enzymes, particularly hyaluronidase, that are released when the sperm approaches the egg. These enzymes aid in breaking down the protective layers surrounding the egg, such as the zona pellucida. The penetration of the egg's protective layers allows the sperm to fuse with the egg's plasma membrane, initiating the process of fertilization.
This option is incorrect. The organelle that plays a crucial role in helping the sperm penetrate the ovum (egg) during fertilization is the acrosome.

questions.explanation: The organelle that plays a crucial role in helping the sperm penetrate the ovum (egg) during fertilization is the acrosome. The acrosome is a specialized structure located at the tip of the sperm cell's head. The acrosome is a vesicle filled with hydrolytic enzymes, particularly hyaluronidase, that are released when the sperm approaches the egg. These enzymes aid in breaking down the protective layers surrounding the egg, such as the zona pellucida. The penetration of the egg's protective layers allows the sperm to fuse with the egg's plasma membrane, initiating the process of fertilization.

questions.text: The uterine tube opens into:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The uterine tube, also known as the fallopian tube, opens into the uterus. Specifically, the distal end of each fallopian tube is connected to the upper part of the uterus. This connection allows for the passage of eggs (ova) from the ovaries into the fallopian tubes and facilitates the transport of fertilized eggs (zygotes) from the fallopian tubes into the uterus for potential implantation.
Ovaries are female gonads which besides producing ova also produce female sex hormones i.e oestrogen and progesterone.
Cervix is the narrow entry of the uterus from vagina which is usually blocked by a plug of mucus.
Oviduct is another name for fallopian tubes or uterine tubes.

questions.explanation: The uterine tube, also known as the fallopian tube, opens into the uterus. Specifically, the distal end of each fallopian tube is connected to the upper part of the uterus. This connection allows for the passage of eggs (ova) from the ovaries into the fallopian tubes and facilitates the transport of fertilized eggs (zygotes) from the fallopian tubes into the uterus for potential implantation.

questions.text: Which hormone is produced mainly by corpus luteum in the ovary following ovulation?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The hormone produced mainly by the corpus luteum in the ovary following ovulation is progesterone. The corpus luteum forms from the ruptured follicle that released an egg during ovulation. After ovulation, the empty follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, and its primary function is to produce progesterone.
In women, FSH helps manage the menstrual cycle and stimulates the ovaries to produce eggs.

In males, LH stimulates testosterone release by the Leydig cells of the testes. In females, LH stimulates steroid release from the ovaries, ovulation, and the release of progesterone after ovulation by the corpus luteum.

Human chorionic gonadotropin (hCG) is a hormone produced by the placenta during pregnancy. It's sometimes called the pregnancy hormone because of its unique role in supporting a pregnancy. HCG is found in your urine or blood around 10 to 11 days after conception.

questions.explanation: The hormone produced mainly by the corpus luteum in the ovary following ovulation is progesterone. The corpus luteum forms from the ruptured follicle that releases an egg during ovulation. After ovulation, the empty follicle transforms into the corpus luteum, and its primary function is to produce progesterone.

questions.text: The end or complete stop of the menstrual cycle is called?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The release of an egg from an ovary during the menstrual cycle is called ovulation. Hence, it is an incorrect option.
Periodic discharge of blood and tissue from the uterus is called menstruation. Hence, it is an incorrect option.
Fusion of gametes to give rise to a new individual organism or offspring is called fertilization. Hence, it is an incorrect option.
Menopause is the time that marks the end of the menstrual cycles of a woman.

questions.explanation: Menopause is the time that marks the end of the menstrual cycles of a woman.

questions.text: Which is the largest cell in the human body?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: While macrophages are relatively large cells involved in the immune system and can be larger than typical cells, they are not the largest cell in the human body.
The human egg, or ovum, is the largest cell in the human body. It is a single, large cell produced by the female reproductive system.
Granule cells are a type of neuron found in the cerebellum of the brain. They are not the largest cell in the human body.
This is not the correct option.

questions.explanation: The human egg, or ovum, is the largest cell in the human body. It is a single, large cell produced by the female reproductive system.

questions.text: Which one of the following does not occur at puberty in girls?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct, as it is the one that does not occur at puberty in girls. In females, the voice may become slightly fuller or more mature due to overall body growth, but it does not deepen significantly. Deepening of the voice is a characteristic of male puberty caused by testosterone, which enlarges the larynx (voice box) and thickens the vocal cords. In girls, the larynx does not grow to the same extent, and their voice remains relatively high-pitched. Therefore, a deep voice is not a feature of female puberty..

This option is incorrect, as it does occur during puberty in girls. The growth of pubic and underarm hair is one of the earliest signs of puberty, stimulated by increasing levels of sex hormones, particularly estrogens and androgens. These hormones cause the appearance of hair in the genital and underarm regions, signaling the body’s transition into sexual maturity.

This option is also incorrect, because broadening of the hips is a normal part of female puberty. It is caused by estrogen, which influences the shape of the female pelvis to prepare the body for possible childbearing. The widening of hips gives the body a more curved and mature appearance, distinguishing the female body structure from that of males.

This option is incorrect as well, since the onset of menstruation (menarche) is one of the major events marking puberty in girls. It occurs as a result of hormonal changes regulated by the pituitary gland and ovaries, mainly through FSH and estrogen. The menstrual cycle represents the start of reproductive maturity, meaning that the ovaries have begun releasing eggs and the uterus is preparing for potential pregnancy.

questions.explanation: In females, puberty is associated with the development of secondary sexual characteristics, including breast development, widening of the hips, and the onset of menstruation. These changes are primarily driven by the production of estrogen and progesterone. Puberty does not typically cause the voice to deepen to the extent seen in males. The deepening of the voice is a characteristic feature of male puberty due to the lengthening and thickening of the vocal cords, which are influenced by increased levels of testosterone.

questions.text: A placenta is established between the uterine and _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct. The placenta forms a vital connection between the uterine wall of the mother and the foetal tissues of the developing baby. It allows the exchange of gases, nutrients, and waste materials between the maternal and foetal blood without the two blood supplies mixing directly. Through this connection, the foetus receives oxygen and nutrients necessary for growth while waste products like carbon dioxide are transported back to the mother for removal. Thus, the placenta acts as both a lifeline and a protective barrier for the developing embryo or foetus throughout pregnancy

This option is incorrect. The placenta does not connect to or involve bone tissues in any way. Bone tissues are part of the skeletal system, which provides structural support and protection but has no role in the development or nourishment of the foetus. The formation of the placenta occurs in the uterus and involves soft tissues such as the uterine lining (endometrium) and the outer layer of the embryonic cells (trophoblasts). Therefore, bone tissues have no contribution to the placental connection or its functions.

This option is also incorrect. Lymph tissues are components of the immune system responsible for fighting infections and maintaining fluid balance in the body. The placenta is not established between the uterine and lymph tissues; rather, it involves uterine and foetal tissues. Although the placenta performs some immune-related roles—such as protecting the foetus from harmful substances—it does not interact directly with the lymphatic tissues. Its structure and function are purely reproductive, not immune-based.

This option is incorrect as well. Cardiac muscle tissue is found only in the heart, where it functions to pump blood throughout the body. The placenta does not form any connection with cardiac muscles because it develops exclusively within the uterine cavity during pregnancy. Its attachment occurs between the uterine wall and foetal membranes, not the heart. Therefore, while the heart indirectly supports the placenta by circulating blood, it is not a part of the placental structure itself.

questions.explanation: The placenta is a temporary organ that forms during pregnancy and serves as the interface between the maternal and fetal tissues. It is established by the merging of maternal and fetal tissues within the uterus. The placenta is a vital structure that plays a crucial role in supporting the developing fetus.

questions.text: A human cell from the ovary has 22 chromosomes and an X chromosome. It is which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: In humans, each cell nucleus contains 23 pairs of chromosomes, a total of 46 chromosomes. The first 22 pairs are called autosomes. The X chromosome is always present as the 23rd chromosome in the ovum or egg, while either an X or a Y chromosome can be present in individual sperm. Since we already know it is present in the ovary of a female so the correct Option is A.
This option is not correct. Sperm cells are male gametes and carry 22 chromosomes and either an X or Y chromosome. A sperm with 22 chromosomes and an X chromosome would be an abnormal gamete.

This option is not correct. Somatic cells are any cells in the body other than the reproductive cells (gametes). They contain the full complement of chromosomes (46 in humans) and come in pairs, with one set inherited from each parent.

This option is correct, but it is too general. A gamete is a reproductive cell (sperm or egg) that contains half the usual number of chromosomes (haploid number) found in somatic cells. In the case of the given human cell from the ovary, it is specifically an egg (female gamete) with 22 chromosomes and an X chromosome.

questions.explanation: In humans, each cell nucleus contains 23 pairs of chromosomes, a total of 46 chromosomes. The first 22 pairs are called autosomes. The X chromosome is always present as the 23rd chromosome in the ovum or egg, while either an X or a Y chromosome can be present in individual sperm. Since we already know it is present in the ovary of a female so the correct Option is A.

The perimetrium is the outermost layer of the uterus and has no connection to the transformation of follicle cells post-ovulation.

After ovulation, the follicle cells transform into the corpus luteum, a structure crucial for secreting hormones like progesterone, which are vital for supporting the uterine lining and potential pregnancy.

The corpus luteum plays a crucial role in the menstrual cycle and early pregnancy. The corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine structure that develops from the ruptured follicle. It secretes hormones, primarily progesterone and some estrogen, which are essential for the maintenance of the uterine lining (endometrium) during the second half of the menstrual cycle. These hormones help prepare the uterus for a potential pregnancy.

questions.text: The human menstrual cycle generally repeats after how many days?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The average menstrual cycle length is 28 days, but it can normally range from 21 to 35 days in different individuals.

questions.text: End of menstrual cycle in old age is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Andropause is a term used to describe a gradual decline in testosterone levels in men as they age. This decline can cause a variety of symptoms, such as decreased sex drive, erectile dysfunction, fatigue, decreased muscle mass, and mood changes. However, unlike menopause, andropause is not a universal experience in men and it is still a subject of debate among medical professionals. Men who are experiencing symptoms of andropause should talk to their healthcare provider to determine the underlying cause and explore treatment options.

Gametopause is a term used to describe the natural decline in reproductive function that occurs in both men and women as they age. In women, this decline is marked by the end of menstrual cycles and the cessation of ovulation, which is known as menopause. In men, the decline is marked by a gradual decrease in testosterone levels and a decline in sperm production. While gametopause is a normal part of aging, it can cause a variety of symptoms and health concerns. Women who experience symptoms of menopause should talk to their healthcare provider about treatment options, while men who are experiencing symptoms of andropause should also consult a healthcare provider.

Menopause is a natural biological process that marks the end of a woman's menstrual cycle. It usually occurs between the ages of 45 and 55, but can occur earlier or later. During menopause, the ovaries stop releasing eggs and the production of hormones such as estrogen and progesterone declines. This can cause a variety of symptoms, such as hot flashes, night sweats, vaginal dryness, mood changes, and sleep disturbances. Menopause also increases the risk of certain health conditions such as osteoporosis and heart disease. While menopause is a normal part of aging, it is important to talk to a healthcare provider about any symptoms or concerns.
This option is incorrect as the correct answer is C.

questions.explanation: Menopause is a natural biological process that marks the end of a woman's menstrual cycle. It usually occurs between the ages of 45 and 55 but can occur earlier or later. During menopause, the ovaries stop releasing eggs and the production of hormones such as estrogen and progesterone declines. This can cause a variety of symptoms, such as hot flashes, night sweats, vaginal dryness, mood changes, and sleep disturbances. Menopause also increases the risk of certain health conditions such as osteoporosis and heart disease. While menopause is a normal part of aging, it is important to talk to a healthcare provider about any symptoms or concerns.

questions.text: After fertilization the zygote increases in size and travels down the fallopian tube to become embedded in the walls of the womb. This process is called:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Ovulation refers to the process during which a mature egg (ovum) is released from the ovary into the fallopian tube. It occurs roughly in the middle of a woman’s menstrual cycle. Ovulation happens before fertilization, not after it. Once the egg is released, it can be fertilized by a sperm cell, but ovulation itself has nothing to do with the zygote or its movement to the uterus.

This option is correct. After fertilization occurs in the fallopian tube, the resulting zygote undergoes several mitotic divisions to form a blastocyst (a hollow ball of cells). This blastocyst then travels down the fallopian tube to reach the uterus (womb). Once there, it attaches itself to and embeds within the endometrial lining of the uterus — a process known as implantation. Implantation marks the beginning of pregnancy, as it allows the developing embryo to receive oxygen and nutrients from the mother’s blood through the uterine lining.

This option is partially correct but not the best answer. Conception refers to the moment of fertilization, when a sperm cell successfully penetrates and fuses with an egg cell in the fallopian tube to form a zygote. However, the question describes the process after fertilization, when the zygote becomes embedded in the uterine wall. Therefore, the correct term for that stage is implantation, not conception.

This option is incorrect. Menstruation (period) is the shedding of the uterine lining (endometrium) when fertilization and implantation do not occur. It marks the end of one menstrual cycle and the beginning of another. Menstruation is the opposite of implantation, as it occurs when there is no pregnancy.

questions.explanation: After fertilization, the zygote undergoes a series of cell divisions, forming a structure known as a blastocyst. The blastocyst then travels down the fallopian tube and eventually reaches the uterus. The process by which the blastocyst becomes embedded in the walls of the uterus is called implantation. Implantation generally occurs around 6-10 days after fertilization. The embryo, at this stage, is a multicellular structure, and the process of implantation marks the beginning of the embryonic stage of development. Once implanted, the cells of the blastocyst continue to differentiate, and the early structures of the developing fetus begin to form.

questions.text: When a female ovulates, in which phase of the division is the oocyte?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: During anaphase I, the homologous chromosomes are separated and move to opposite poles of the cell. This does not occur during ovulation, so this is not the correct answer.

During prophase I, the chromosomes condense and the nuclear envelope breaks down. This does occur during ovulation, but it is only the first stage of meiosis. The oocyte is in metaphase II of meiosis during ovulation, so this is not the correct answer.

During metaphase I, the chromosomes line up along the middle of the cell. This does occur during ovulation, but it is only the second stage of meiosis. The oocyte is in metaphase II of meiosis during ovulation, so this is not the correct answer.
After ovulation, the oocyte is arrested in metaphase of meiosis II until fertilization.

questions.explanation: After ovulation, the oocyte is arrested in metaphase of meiosis II until fertilization.

questions.text: In 'cloning', nucleus is introduced into which of the following?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The nucleus is inserted into an egg cell during cloning. Cloning is removing the nucleus from an egg cell and replacing it with a somatic cell from the animal that is being cloned, such as a skin or blood cell. The resulting repaired egg cell is stimulated to divide and form an embryo because it now has the entire set of genetic instructions from the donor animal.

The nucleus is inserted into an egg cell during cloning. Cloning is removing the nucleus from an egg cell and replacing it with a somatic cell from the animal that is being cloned, such as a skin or blood cell. The resulting repaired egg cell is stimulated to divide and form an embryo because it now has the entire set of genetic instructions from the donor animal.
Sperm cells only contain half of the genetic information needed to form a complete organism, so they cannot be used on their own to create a cloned animal.

Zygotes, which are formed by the fusion of a sperm cell and an egg cell during fertilization, contain the complete set of genetic instructions for an organism. However, they are not typically used in the process of cloning because they have already undergone some degree of development and differentiation.

questions.explanation: Option B is correct since in animals and especially among vertebrates, a nucleus from somatic cells is removed and introduced into an egg cell, whose own nucleus is destroyed by ultraviolet radiations.Moreover, through this method, the egg develops into an organism, genetically identical to the parent who has contributed the nucleus.

questions.text: Where in the female reproductive system are thousands of eggs found?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Thousands of eggs are found in the ovaries of the female reproductive system.

The vagina is a part of the female reproductive system, but it does not contain thousands of eggs. The vagina is a muscular canal that connects the external genitalia to the cervix of the uterus and serves as the entry point for the penis during sexual intercourse and the exit point for menstrual flow.

The fallopian tubes, also known as oviducts, are a pair of slender tubes that extend from the ovaries to the uterus. They provide a pathway for the eggs to travel from the ovaries to the uterus after ovulation. While the fallopian tubes play a crucial role in fertilization, they do not store thousands of eggs.
The uterus, also known as the womb, is the organ where a fertilized egg implants and develops into a fetus during pregnancy. It is not the site where thousands of eggs are found.

questions.explanation: Option A is correct since thousands of eggs are found in the ovaries of the female reproductive system.The ovaries are a pair of small, almond-shaped organs located on either side of the uterus in the female reproductive system.

questions.text: The follicle cells, after release of the egg, are modified to form a special structure called:

After ovulation, the follicle cells transform into the corpus luteum, a structure crucial for secreting hormones like progesterone, which are vital for supporting the uterine lining and potential pregnancy.

The corpus luteum plays a crucial role in the menstrual cycle and early pregnancy. The corpus luteum is a temporary endocrine structure that develops from the ruptured follicle. It secretes hormones, primarily progesterone and some estrogen, which are essential for the maintenance of the uterine lining (endometrium) during the second half of the menstrual cycle. questions.text: During the menstrual cycle, which of the following events happens if a released egg does not become fertilized?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: The lining of the womb wall stays built up
options.explanation: This option is not correct because the lining of the womb wall, also known as the endometrium, breaks down during menstruation.

options.text: The lining of the womb wall builds up again
options.explanation: This option is also not correct because the lining of the womb wall needs to break down first before it can build up again in preparation for a potential pregnancy.
This option is also not correct because the release of an egg from the ovary, also known as ovulation, typically occurs once per menstrual cycle, regardless of whether or not the egg is fertilized.

options.text: The lining of the womb wall breaks down
options.explanation: During the menstrual cycle, if a released egg does not become fertilized, the lining of the womb wall breaks down. This is known as menstruation and marks the beginning of a new menstrual cycle.

questions.explanation: During the menstrual cycle, if a released egg does not become fertilized, the lining of the womb wall breaks down. This is known as menstruation and marks the beginning of a new menstrual cycle.

questions.text: The period during which a girl sexually matures is called _.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Menstrual cycle refers to the monthly cycle of changes that occur in a woman's body in preparation for pregnancy, including the shedding of the uterine lining and the release of an egg from the ovaries.
Puberty is the stage of adolescence where hormones for secondary sex characters are released and a girl sexually matures.
Childhood refers to the period of life before puberty.

Teens, or adolescence, is a broader term that refers to the period of life between childhood and adulthood, generally between the ages of 13 and 19, during which many significant physical, cognitive, and social changes occur.

questions.explanation: Puberty is the stage of adolescence where hormones for secondary sex characters are released and a girl sexually matures.

questions.text: The total gestation period (pregnancy) is usually about:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This duration is much shorter than the average gestation period for humans. A pregnancy lasting only 28 days would be significantly shorter than normal and not sufficient for the development of a full-term fetus.

This option is closer to the average duration of human pregnancy compared to the previous option, it still falls short of the typical 280-day gestation period. A 250-day pregnancy would be shorter than average.
The gestation period refers to the length of time from conception to birth. In humans, the average duration of a full-term pregnancy is approximately 40 weeks or 280 days.

This option suggests a longer gestation period than what is typically observed in most human pregnancies. While some pregnancies may extend beyond 280 days, a 300-day pregnancy would be longer than average.

questions.explanation: The gestation period refers to the length of time from conception to birth. In humans, the average duration of a full-term pregnancy is approximately 40 weeks or 280 days.

questions.text: In which organism males are haploid?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option suggests that no organisms listed have haploid males, which is incorrect.
Mosquitoes are diploid organisms, meaning that both males and females have two sets of chromosomes.
Similar to mosquitoes, butterflies are also diploid, with both males and females possessing a full set of chromosomes.
In honey bees, males, known as drones, are haploid. They develop from unfertilized eggs and possess only one set of chromosomes, which is a unique reproductive strategy.

questions.explanation: In honey bees, males (drones) are haploid, meaning they carry only one set of chromosomes (16 chromosomes in total) derived from their mother, the diploid queen, who has 32 chromosomes. This haploid nature allows them to produce sperm that is genetically identical to themselves, apart from any mutations. In contrast, mosquitoes and butterflies are diploid organisms where both sexes possess two sets of chromosomes, thus making options B and C incorrect. Option A is also incorrect as honey bees do have haploid males.
